one_line_takeaway: Abstract Gradient Training certifies an unmodified SGD-trained network against bounded/unbounded poisoning and backdoor adversaries by propagating sound parameter-space intervals (via an interval-parameter CROWN extension), trading tightness for the ability to analyze any first-order training run without changing it.
key_finding: AGT produces non-vacuous certified guarantees on unmodified networks across regression and binary/medical/autonomous-driving tasks, but its bounds are inherently loose because between iterations they assume worst-case poisoning at every parameter index simultaneously (an adversary no real attack can realize), so useful guarantees require large batch sizes and/or few epochs. Concretely, on MNIST label-flipping AGT can beat randomized smoothing only for a small number of flips and never beats Deep Partition Aggregation (DPA) - but unlike DPA (which needs a 3000-model ensemble, i.e. ~3000x the space cost, and modifies training) and unlike RS (which only applies to linear models / label flipping), AGT leaves the training algorithm untouched and handles general adversaries. CROWN-based bounds are tighter than IBP for deeper networks while IBP can win for shallow-wide ones.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Abstract Gradient Training (AGT), which the authors present as the first framework giving provable, sound guarantees on the behavior of models trained on potentially poisoned data WITHOUT modifying the model or the learning algorithm. Instead of building a robust learner (the approach of prior partition/smoothing defenses it contrasts itself against, e.g. DPA and randomized smoothing), AGT analyzes the sensitivity of an unmodified first-order (SGD/Adam) training run: it uses convex relaxations to over-approximate the set of all parameter updates an adversary could induce, maintaining a sound interval [theta_L, theta_U] over all reachable parameters across every training iteration. A central theorem (Thm 3.2) shows that bounding any adversarial goal J can be done by maximizing over this parameter interval rather than over the intractable space of poisoned datasets. A key technical ingredient the paper claims as novel is an explicit extension of the CROWN bound-propagation algorithm to the interval-parameter setting (Prop 3.5), where the network weights/biases used in the linear relaxations are themselves intervals.
threat_model: Worst-case data-poisoning and backdoor adversary against gradient-based training. Two settings: (1) BOUNDED adversary <n, eps, p, nu, q> may perturb up to n training points, changing features by at most eps in l_p norm and labels by at most nu in l_q norm (label-flipping is the special case nu=1, q=0); (2) UNBOUNDED adversary may add/remove up to n arbitrary points, which requires per-sample gradient clipping (parameter kappa) to keep bounds finite. The adversary is assumed omniscient and white-box: full access to architecture, initialization, data, data ordering, and hyperparameters. Three adversarial goals are certified: untargeted (degrade overall accuracy / denial-of-service), targeted (misclassify specific inputs), and backdoor (implant a trigger while keeping clean accuracy stable). Important scoping caveat stated by the authors: n is a bound on poisoned points PER BATCH, not per dataset.
limitations: Bounds are sound but often loose: the per-index worst-case assumption is unrealizable, forcing atypical training regimes (large batches, few epochs) to stay non-vacuous. Multi-class cross-entropy has very loose interval relaxations, so multi-class problems get weaker guarantees than regression or binary classification (many experiments are cast as binary). The n-poisoned bound is per-batch not per-dataset. Exact solutions to the underlying NP-hard gradient-bounding problem (Eq. 8) are left to future work (e.g. MIP). Bound tightness is highly sensitive to architecture, width, depth, learning rate, and batch size. The medical backdoor model is fragile even without poisoning (eps=0.009 already drops certified backdoor accuracy below 50%), reflecting inference-time verification looseness rather than the training bound alone.

datasets_used: halfmoons (toy binary classification, 128-neuron hidden layer, for visualization)
UCI Individual Household Electric Power Consumption ('houseelectric') - regression, MSE loss
MNIST (label-flipping baseline comparison; projected to 32-dim via PCA)
OCTMNIST / MedMNIST retinal OCT (normal vs abnormal binary classification; Drusen as poisoned fine-tuning class)
Udacity self-driving car dataset with PilotNet (steering-angle regression, autonomous driving)
quantitative_results: MNIST label-flipping: AGT beats randomized smoothing only for a small number of flips and NEVER beats DPA; DPA used a 3000-model ensemble (~3000x space cost vs standard training).
Runtime: AGT typically < 2x standard training (Appendix G).
Complexity: AGT with IBP is O(b*m*n^2) per batch (equivalent to standard training); with CROWN bounds it is O(b*m^2*n^3) per batch; original CROWN is O(m^2*n^3).
OCTMNIST feature-only poisoning: attack > eps=0.02 over n~500 samples yields bounds worse than the original pre-trained accuracy (batch b=6000 with 3000 Drusen/batch).

OCTMNIST label-only poisoning: needs n>=600 poisoned samples (20% of Drusen per batch) before the accuracy bound degrades to the pre-trained model's accuracy - i.e. label poisoning certifies more strongly here.
OCTMNIST backdoor: only eps=0.009 reduces certified backdoor accuracy below 50% (model fragile to inference-time perturbation even without poisoning).
halfmoons: label poisoning gives much looser bounds than feature poisoning - n=5 label flips ~ n=200 feature perturbations in bound width (due to full-width {0,1} label interval).
PilotNet fine-tuning: certified MSE roughly 0.02-0.04 on Udacity video 1 as labels-poisoned n grows to ~600 (illustrative point n=300, nu=0.01).
CROWN vs IBP (UCI): CROWN yields tighter average parameter-interval width for deeper models; IBP can be tighter for shallow/wide models; interval/CROWN bounds weaken roughly exponentially with depth.
n=10000 on UCI houseelectric corresponds to 100% of the dataset being potentially poisoned.
baselines_compared: Deep Partition Aggregation (DPA), Levine & Feizi 2020 - AGT never outperforms it, but DPA needs a 3000-model ensemble and modifies the learner
Randomized Smoothing (RS), Rosenfeld et al. 2020 - AGT beats it for few label flips; RS only applies to linear models / label-flipping
IBP (interval bound propagation) as an internal alternative to the paper's CROWN-style bounds (element-wise min of both is used)
